title: Safe Practices and Wildcards
description: Avoid disasters with interactive deletion and bulk operations using wildcards.

slides.title: The Star of the Show: The Asterisk `*` ⭐
slides.content:
# Wildcards: Selecting Multiple Files

Sometimes you want to delete *all* `.txt` files, or copy *all* `.jpg` photos. Typing every name is exhausting.

Enter the **Wildcard** character: `*` (asterisk).

It means **'match anything'**.

### Examples:
*   **`*.txt`**: All files ending in `.txt`.
*   **`report*`**: All files starting with `report` (e.g., `report1.doc`, `report-final.pdf`).
*   **`*.*`**: All files that have a dot in the name (not very useful, but valid).
*   **`*`**: **EVERY SINGLE FILE AND FOLDER** in the current directory.

slides.title: Wildcard in Action: Copying
slides.content: Copy all text files from current directory to a backup folder.
slides.codeSnippet:
mkdir backup
cp *.txt backup/
ls backup/

quizData.question: Which pattern matches files named 'data1.csv', 'data2.csv', and 'data_backup.csv'?
quizData.explanation: `data*.csv` means 'start with data, then anything, then end with .csv'.

slides.title: ⚠️ Danger: `rm *`
slides.content:
# The Nuclear Keyboard Slip

Imagine you want to delete all `.tmp` files:

`rm *.tmp`

What if you accidentally hit the **Spacebar** before the asterisk?

`rm * .tmp`

**TRANSLATION:** Delete **EVERYTHING** in the current folder (`*`), and then also delete the file `.tmp`.

You have just wiped out your entire current directory.

### The Golden Rule of `rm`:
**ALWAYS** run `ls` with the same pattern FIRST to see what you are about to delete.

```bash
ls *.tmp   # Check what matches
rm *.tmp   # Only run this if the list looks correct
```

slides.title: The Question Mark Wildcard `?` ❓
slides.content:
# Match Exactly One Character

While `*` matches **zero or more** characters, `?` matches **exactly one** character.

### Example:
You have files: `file1.txt`, `file2.txt`, `file10.txt`.

*   **`ls file?.txt`**: Matches `file1.txt` and `file2.txt` (only one character after 'file').
*   **`ls file*.txt`**: Matches all three (any number of characters after 'file').

This is useful for structured file naming (like logs: `access.log.1`, `access.log.2`).

quizData.question: What is the safest way to use a wildcard with `rm`?

options.text: Just type rm *.txt and hope for the best.
Use `ls` with the same pattern first to preview.

quizData.explanation: Previewing with `ls` allows you to see exactly which files the shell will expand the wildcard to include.

slides.title: Alias to the Rescue 🚀
slides.content:
## Make `rm` Always Ask

Many Linux distributions automatically set an **alias** that makes `rm` behave like `rm -i` (interactive) by default.

You can check if you have this safety net by typing:
```bash
alias rm
```
If it says `alias rm='rm -i'`, you are protected. You will be prompted before deleting every file.

To override this safety and force delete anyway (DANGER), you use `\rm` or `rm -f`.
